consensus_findings.description: Design.md Section 'Integration Points' and Spec Requirement 'Session Log Committed with Phase Artifacts' incorrectly classify iterate-on-plan and iterate-on-implementation as 'skills that don't normally commit'. Both skills already have git add/commit steps (iterate-on-plan commits to openspec/changes/$CHANGE_ID/, iterate-on-implementation commits with git add .). This means session-log.md should be included in their existing commits, not added as a separate dedicated commit.

The sanitize-then-verify flow runs sanitization in-place (same path for input and output). While this works because Python reads the entire file before writing, the sanitize_session_log.py script was not designed or documented for in-place use. The 'input' and 'output' arguments are separate positional args with no documented support for same-path usage. If the script implementation changes to stream reads, in-place would break silently.

The append_phase_entry() and append_merge_entry() Python functions add a code dependency between all 7 workflow skills and the session-log infrastructure module. Currently skills are loosely coupled — they reference scripts via relative paths in bash. The new functions require Python imports, which means skills need the session-log module on sys.path. This works in the main repo but may not work in all worktree configurations or when skills are synced to other runtimes.

Merge Log Artifact requirement has no scenario for merge sessions with zero PRs processed (e.g., all PRs are drafts or all fail CI). Should a merge-log entry still be written? An empty triage table with an observation like 'No actionable PRs found' is still useful context for understanding why nothing was merged.

The 80-line-per-entry and 500-line-total limits in the Content Structure requirement use SHALL but provide an escape hatch ('unless the agent documents a rationale'). This is effectively SHOULD with documentation. The inconsistency may confuse strict validators. Consider either making them SHOULD or removing the escape hatch.

The plan does not address what happens to the existing session-log test file (test_extract_session_log.py) for functions that are being kept (generate_self_summary_prompt). Task 1.3 says 'remove tests for retired Tier 1/2 functions' but doesn't mention keeping existing tests for generate_self_summary_prompt().

Task 6.1 (final validation) depends on all skill tasks (2.1-5.1), but the delta spec was already written during planning. Task 6.1 is really 'run validation and fix issues', not 'write the spec'. The dependency is correct for validation timing but the task description could be clearer.

design.md says concurrent session-log.md edits will be combined during merge_worktrees.py and true conflicts will be resolved chronologically, but the task list never updates merge_worktrees.py or its tests. The current integrator aborts on merge conflicts, so the parallel-agent path described by the spec has no implementation route.

The proposal explicitly keeps sanitize_session_log.py unchanged while expanding committed artifacts to merge logs that can contain arbitrary PR comments, vendor-review text, and user-provided content. The plan adds no task to broaden secret-pattern coverage or add merge-log-specific regression tests, so the claim that committed logs cannot leak secrets is not supported by the implementation plan.

Tasks 3.2 and 4.1 require iterate-on-implementation and validate-feature to commit session-log.md, but the existing worktree-aware skills already resolve OpenSpec artifacts via $OPENSPEC_PATH outside the active worktree when running in worktree mode. The plan does not specify how these new writes will stay on the feature branch, so the design does not align with the current worktree-isolation pattern.

The delta spec is inconsistent about session IDs: the phase-append scenario requires every entry to identify a session ID, but the content-structure requirement and the design template treat session ID as optional. Agents without a stable session identifier will not know whether omitting it is compliant.

Task 1.3 adds new tests for the refactored session-log helpers, but the only validation task is openspec validate --strict. No task runs the Python test suite for the security-sensitive script refactor, so the plan leaves the highest-risk code path unverified.
